text: The video shows the lower end of the esophagus, the cardia, and the fundus of the stomach, which have migrated into the mediastinum. The surgeon pulls the stomach back to examine the anatomy.

The video shows the lower end of the esophagus, the cardia, and the fundus of the stomach, which have migrated into the mediastinum. The surgeon pulls the stomach back to examine the anatomical structures involved in the type three hiatal hernia.

The surgeon incises the phrenoesophageal membrane above the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve, preserving the nerve while dividing the membrane along the right crus. A surgical plane is being created in the process.

The surgeon incises the phrenoesophageal membrane above the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve, preserving the nerve while dividing the membrane along the right crus. A surgical plane is being created in this area.
The surgeon extends the incision of the phrenoesophageal membrane from the right crus towards the left crus, carefully incising the membrane while avoiding division of the fascia covering the crus.
The surgeon extends the incision of the phrenoesophageal membrane from the right crus towards the left crus, carefully incising the membrane while avoiding division of the fascia covering the crus.

The surgeon carefully divides small attachments between the fundus of the stomach, ensuring the fundus is fully mobilized and free for tension-free fundoplication. Attention is paid to avoid injuring the anteriorly located left vagal branch.

The surgeon carefully divides small attachments between the fundus of the stomach, ensuring the fundus is fully mobilized and free from tension for the upcoming fundoplication procedure. Attention is given to avoid injury to the anteriorly located left vagal branch.

In this patient, because the demister score was very high and in manometry he did not have any other esophageal mortality disorders, we were planning to perform a laparoscopic nascence floppy fundoplication.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the right and left crura and the esophagus, aiming to identify the pleura while avoiding bleeding that could obscure the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the right and left crura and the esophagus, aiming to identify the pleura. The pleura is visibly close to the dissection area, requiring caution to avoid incising it and causing bleeding that could obscure the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully navigates near the left pleura, which is visibly identified as a white line below the instrument. Precautions are taken to avoid incising the pleura, with options to close any larger rents using intracorporeal sutures or an endo loop.

The surgeon identifies the left pleura, visible as a white line below the instrument, and continues the dissection into the mediastinum between the esophagus, aiming to mobilize at least five centimeters of the esophagus intra-abdominally for effective fundoplication.

The surgeon continues the dissection into the mediastinum, carefully separating tissue around the esophagus to mobilize at least five centimeters of the esophagus into the abdominal cavity, aiming to prevent recurrence and provide relief.

The surgeon opens the omentum into two layers, identifying the upper branches of the short gastric vessels. These vessels are coagulated and divided separately to ensure a floppy fundus for the fundoplication, minimizing the risk of bleeding.

The surgeon begins dividing the upper branches of the short gastric vessels to ensure a floppy fundus for the fundoplication. The omentum is opened into two layers, allowing identification and separate coagulation and division of the short gastric vessels to minimize bleeding risk. The omentum is not excessively bulky in this patient, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic omentum in one section, carefully avoiding thermal injury to the stomach by maintaining a safe distance. The dissection continues with the division of the upper short gastric vessels, ensuring secure hemostasis to prevent postoperative bleeding.

The surgeon continues the dissection by dividing the upper short gastric vessels, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the stomach by not working too closely to it. The gastric omentum is divided in a controlled manner to prevent potential bleeding.

The surgeon divides two short gastric vessels near the fundus, avoiding mobilization of the entire greater curvature. The second branch of the short gastric vessel is divided, and small posterior attachments are severed to fully free the fundus, which is now visible.

The surgeon divides two short gastric vessels, avoiding mobilization of the entire greater curvature, to ensure the fundus is free from undue retraction. The second branch of the short gastric vessel is divided, and the small posterior attachments are also severed, allowing the fundus to become visible.

The surgeon carefully holds and divides the last attachments of the fundus, ensuring a few millimeters of tissue remain between the stomach and the dissection site to prevent thermal damage. The dissection is performed cautiously to avoid going too lateral, which could damage the splenic capsule and cause hemorrhage.

The surgeon carefully holds and divides the last attachments of the fundus, ensuring a few millimeters of tissue remain between the stomach and the dissection site to prevent thermal damage. The dissection is performed cautiously to avoid going too lateral, which could damage the splenic capsule and lead to hemorrhage.
The surgeon has freed the entire fundus, dividing the remaining attachments between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach to achieve a floppy fundus suitable for fundoplication.

The surgeon has freed the entire fundus, dividing the remaining attachments between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach. The last tiny vessels are carefully divided to avoid damage to surrounding structures, resulting in a completely detached and floppy fundus ready for fundoplication.
The surgeon divides the last of the tiny vessels near the fundus, ensuring no damage to surrounding structures. The fundus is now completely detached and free from any attachments.

The surgeon takes down the few remaining posterior attachments, then performs posterior dissection on the right side to pass an umbilical tape around the esophagus. The tape is used for safe manipulation of the esophagus, avoiding direct instrument contact due to its edematous state.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the remaining posterior attachments, then performs posterior dissection on the right side to pass an umbilical tape around the esophagus for manipulation. The esophagus appears edematous, and care is taken to avoid direct handling with instruments to prevent damage.

The surgeon places a couple of knots and uses an instrument to manipulate the tissue, ensuring safe handling. The dissection is carried further cranially into the mediastinum, revealing deeper anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection cranially into the mediastinum, revealing the beating heart anterior to the instrument and the pericardium in view. The pleura is clearly identified, and dissection proceeds carefully between the pleura and periesophageal tissues to avoid bleeding.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field with the beating heart visible anterior to the instrument, revealing the pericardium. The pleura is clearly identified, and dissection is carefully carried out between the pleura and perisophageal tissues to avoid bleeding.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to aid in dissection, creating a clear surgical plane. Fibrinous attachments are visible and are sharply divided to prevent bleeding. The dissection progresses into the mediastinum to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring sufficient intra-abdominal length for the fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to aid in dissection, creating a clear surgical plane. Fibrinous attachments are visible and are carefully divided using sharp dissection to prevent bleeding. The surgical planes are opened strategically to facilitate easy dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection into the mediastinum to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring sufficient intra-abdominal length for optimal post-operative results.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection, carefully taking down attachments between the tissue surrounding the pericardium and the esophagus.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection, carefully taking down attachments between the tissue surrounding the pericardium and the esophagus.
The surgeon performs a circumferential resection around the esophagus, carefully taking down fibers near the beating heart, which is visible to the left of the instrument.

The surgeon performs a circumferential mobilization of the esophagus, carefully dissecting fibers around the structure. The beating heart is visible to the left of the instrument, while the pleura is clearly seen on the right side. This comprehensive mobilization ensures adequate esophageal length for the fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon performs circumferential mobilization of the esophagus, achieving a comprehensive dissection length of eleven centimeters. The pleura is visible on both the right and left sides. An umbilical tape, cut to eleven centimeters, is used to measure the mobilized esophageal component, which extends six to six and a half centimeters into the abdomen.

The surgeon uses an 11-centimeter umbilical tape to measure the extent of esophageal dissection, with approximately 6 to 6.5 centimeters mobilized into the abdomen. The posterior plane is further opened, revealing the left crus. Both crura are clearly dissected, and small fibrous tissues between the esophagus and posterior area are carefully removed, ensuring the right vagus nerve remains undamaged.

The surgeon further opens the posterior plane, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm. Both crura are clearly dissected, with small fibrous tissues between the esophagus and the posterior area being carefully removed. Attention is given to avoid damaging the right vagus nerve, which is located slightly posterior.

The surgeon carefully avoids excessive posterior dissection near the visible aorta, while the left and right crura are seen anteriorly. The beating heart and pericardium are visible, and the esophagus has been fully mobilized, highlighting the extensive dissection performed.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the visible aorta, ensuring not to dissect too posteriorly. The left and right crura are visible, with the beating heart and pericardium seen anteriorly. The esophagus is fully mobilized, highlighting the extensive dissection performed for the procedure.
The surgeon takes a suture bite on the left crus, ensuring the needle passes through the fascia rather than the muscle, as the fascia provides greater strength and stability.
The surgeon takes a suture bite on the left crus, ensuring the needle passes through the fascia rather than the muscle to provide adequate strength and prevent cut-through.

The surgeon carefully places sutures in the hiatal area, ensuring not to damage vital structures such as the IVC located outside the right crest laterally. Monofilament sutures are preferred for their handling capacity, although multifilament options like ethyl bond are also commonly used.

The surgeon carefully sutures in the hiatal area, ensuring not to damage vital structures such as the aorta and inferior vena cava, which are located posteriorly and laterally. Monofilament sutures are used for their handling capacity, with attention to avoid excessive tightening that could throttle the tissues.

The surgeon completes the suture at the hiatal area, assessing the space available to ensure the esophagus maintains normal peristalsis. A second hiatal bite is taken, with careful control of the needle tip to avoid damaging the liver, as the assisting hand pushes the liver away.
The surgeon assesses the space available to determine the number of additional sutures needed, ensuring that the esophagus can maintain normal peristalsis without compromise.

The surgeon takes the second hiatal bite, carefully controlling the needle to avoid damaging the liver. The assisting hand is visible, pushing the liver away to provide a clear view of the surgical area.

The surgeon removes the needle and takes another suture bite, similar to previous steps. As the suture is tightened, the small gap between tissues becomes visible. The esophagus is manipulated with umbilical tape, creating the appearance of a larger gap, which could potentially lead to a hiatal hernia.

The surgeon takes a needle bite similar to previous sutures, tightening it to minimize the gap around the esophagus. The umbilical tape manipulation makes the gap appear larger, but once the tape is relaxed, the esophagus is observed in its resting state to assess the adequacy of the crudoplasty.

After completing the suture, the surgeon reduces manipulation on the umbilical tape around the esophagus, allowing it to rest in its natural position to assess the adequacy of the crudoplasty and determine if additional suturing is necessary.

The surgeon demonstrates that an instrument passes freely through the approximated hiatal defect, indicating successful closure. The traction on the umbilical tape is released, revealing minimal space between the esophagus and the hiatus.

The surgeon demonstrates that an instrument passes freely through the hiatal defect, which has been nicely approximated. Upon releasing the traction on the umbilical tape, the esophagus is seen resting with minimal space between it and the hiatus.

The surgeon brings the mobilized gastric fundus to the posterior window created earlier. The fundus, freed by dividing the upper short gastric vessels, is positioned without undue tension, with visible vessels running along its surface.

The surgeon brings the mobilized gastric fundus through the posterior window created earlier. The fundus, freed by dividing the upper short gastric vessels, is visible with its associated vessels. The fundus is positioned without undue tension, and the shoeshine maneuver is performed to ensure its free mobility.

The surgeon performs the shoeshine maneuver to assess the mobility of the fundus, ensuring there is no undue tension. The fundus is then released without any instruments, and it remains in position, indicating successful mobilization.

The surgeon holds the other portion of the fundus and performs the shoeshine maneuver, demonstrating no undue tension. The fundus is then released from the instrument, remaining in its position without retracting, indicating adequate mobilization.

The surgeon takes a suture bite on the anterior wall of the stomach on the patient's left side and a bite on the posterior wall on the right side, ensuring proper alignment to prevent twisting of the wrap.

The surgeon takes a bite on the anterior wall of the stomach on the patient's left side and a bite on the posterior wall on the right side, ensuring proper alignment to prevent twisting of the wrap. The bites are then gradually tightened.

The surgeon tightens the sutures after taking bites on both the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach, preparing for a floppy Nissen fundoplication with a short wrap measuring approximately two to two and a half centimeters.

The surgeon performs a floppy nascent fundoplication, creating a short wrap approximately 2 to 2.5 centimeters in length. The second suture includes the esophageal wall to prevent wrap migration, ensuring the wrap is positioned at the level of the esophagogastric junction or slightly higher.

The surgeon takes the second suture bite, including the esophageal wall to prevent migration of the wrap. The wrap is positioned at the level of the esophagogastric junction or slightly higher. The third suture bite is taken, involving only the walls of the stomach without including the esophagus.

The surgeon takes the third bite for the fundoplication wrap, ensuring it is positioned above the OG junction. This bite includes only the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach, excluding the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully controls the needle tip to prevent damage to the liver while taking a bite. After securing the bite, the wrap is lifted to check for adequate space for instrument passage, demonstrating the floppiness of the wrap. The wrap measures approximately two to two and a half centimeters, which is crucial to minimize the risk of dysphagia.

The surgeon carefully controls the needle tip to prevent damage to the liver while taking a bite. After completing the stitch, the wrap is lifted to check for sufficient space for an instrument to pass through.

The surgeon demonstrates the floppiness of the fundoplication wrap, which measures approximately two to two and a half centimeters. The wrap is visually assessed for its size and tension, ensuring it is not too large to prevent dysphagia. The surgeon considers taking an additional bite from the wrap and the crura to prevent early migration post-operatively.
The surgeon takes an additional bite from the wrap and the crura to prevent early migration of the wrap in the post-operative period, ensuring stability of the surgical repair.
The surgeon anchors the wrap to the right crest, ensuring it is securely positioned. The wrap is then inspected for hemostasis and proper alignment, appearing neat and well-structured.

The surgeon anchors the wrap to the right crus, ensuring it is securely positioned. The wrap is inspected for hemostasis and proper positioning, appearing neat and intact. The surgeon then examines the completed crural repair, confirming its satisfactory condition.
The surgeon pushes the fundoplication wrap into position and examines the crural repair, ensuring it is intact and well-formed.
